foundation.title: STRING
foundation.subtitle: Textual DNA
foundation.content: Strings are sequences of characters. In Python, they are immutable—this means once a string is born, it cannot be edited. It can only be reborn as a new string.
foundation.scenario: A Name Tag. If you write 'Neo' and want it to say 'Nio', you can't erase a letter (Immutability). You must throw the tag away and write a new one.

examples.title: Creation
examples.explanation: Defining text using either single or double quotes.

examples.title: Character Access
examples.explanation: Retrieving a single character using its 0-indexed position.

examples.title: Index Logic
examples.explanation: Using negative numbers to count backwards from the end (-1 is the last).

foundation.keyPoint: Strings are IMMUTABLE. You cannot do: s[0] = 'X'.

foundation.title: SLICE
foundation.subtitle: String Slicing
foundation.content: Slicing allows you to extract a specific 'chunk' of text from a larger string using the [start:stop] syntax.
foundation.scenario: A Subway Sandwich. You don't always want the whole 12 inches. You take a slice from 4 inches to 8 inches to get exactly what you need.

examples.title: Basic Slice
examples.explanation: Extracting text starting at index 2 up to (but not including) index 5.

examples.title: The Shortcut
examples.explanation: Leaving one side empty means 'from the beginning' or 'to the end'.

examples.title: Step Slice
examples.explanation: Using a third number to skip characters (e.g. every second char).

foundation.title: FUNCTION
foundation.subtitle: The Reusable Machine
foundation.content: A function is a pre-built machine. You build it once, give it a name, and press its button (Call it) whenever you need that job done.
foundation.scenario: A Coffee Machine. You don't build the circuit every morning. You just press the 'Brew' button (Call), and out comes a result.

examples.title: The Definition
examples.explanation: Creating the blueprint for your machine using the 'def' keyword.

examples.title: The Call
examples.explanation: Running the code inside the function by typing its name with ().

examples.title: Scope Isolation
examples.explanation: Variables created inside a function are private and don't exist outside.
examples.code:
def f(): x = 5
print(x) # Error: x is local!

foundation.keyPoint: def name(): defines it. name() runs it.

foundation.title: PARAMS
foundation.subtitle: Machine Inputs
foundation.content: Parameters are holes in your machine where you pour data (Arguments) to change how the machine behaves.
foundation.scenario: An Electric Oven. The 'Temperature' is a parameter. You set it to 200 (Argument) to bake pizza, or 50 to keep it warm.

examples.title: Single Input
examples.explanation: Passing one piece of data to become a variable inside the function.

examples.title: Multi-Input
examples.explanation: Providing multiple data points separated by commas.

examples.title: Default Value
examples.explanation: Giving a parameter a 'Plan B' value if the user forgets to provide one.

foundation.keyPoint: Arguments are the actual data. Parameters are the names used in the def.

foundation.title: RETURN
foundation.subtitle: Machine Results
foundation.content: Return is the output slot of your machine. It hands data back to the rest of the program so you can use it later.
foundation.scenario: A Cashier at a Store. You give money (Input), they process the transaction, and they RETURN your receipt and change (Output).

examples.title: Basic Return
examples.explanation: Sending a calculation result back to the main code line.
examples.code:
def cube(x):
    return x * x * x

examples.title: Save to Variable
examples.explanation: Capturing the returned value so you can store it in a new box.

examples.title: The Exit
examples.explanation: 'return' immediately kills the function. No code after return ever runs.

foundation.keyPoint: return exits the function and hands back data.

gameData.q: Valid way to define a function?
